title: Long-lived local quantum coherences from hydrodynamic large deviations
authors: Ewan McCulloch, J. Alexander Jacoby, Sarang Gopalakrishnan
abstract: We develop a framework to describe how quantum coherences between distinct charge sectors evolve under generic charge-conserving dynamics. Our framework captures the nonperturbative interactions between quantum coherences and hydrodynamic large deviations -- i.e., rare ``voids'' of low charge entropy. Conditional on surviving, the quantum coherence and its surrounding void form a collective polaron-like object. In one dimension, even at infinite temperature, we show that the lifetime of coherences is parametrically enhanced because they bind to voids. We use our framework to address two fundamental questions about generic quantum dynamics with a conserved charge. First, we argue that gapped Ruelle-Pollicott resonances are absent in the weak-noise limit, even in sectors of operator space that contain no hydrodynamic slow modes: instead, the spectral gap in all sectors vanishes nonperturbatively in the noise strength. Second, we compute the spacetime asymptotics of the dynamical single-particle Green's function, both in the weak-noise regime and in the absence of noise. In the noiseless case, we find that the void-coherence polaron undergoes subdiffusion, with an exponent we calculate. We support our general arguments with a microscopic derivation for random charge-conserving circuits, as well as numerical evidence from tensor-network simulations.
summary: The paper describes how local quantum coherences in charge-conserving systems are protected from decay by binding to rare, low-entropy hydrodynamic fluctuations called 'voids.' This binding creates a 'polaron-like' object that significantly extends the lifetime of the coherence. The authors provide a complete hydrodynamic theory and verify it with numerical simulations of random circuits.
main_problem: Understanding how local quantum coherences between distinct charge sectors evolve and decay under generic charge-conserving dynamics, specifically investigating their interaction with hydrodynamic large deviations.
main_result: The authors show that quantum coherences bind to hydrodynamic 'voids' of low charge entropy, forming a collective 'void-coherence polaron' that enhances coherence lifetime; in 1D, the spectral gap vanishes nonperturbatively as the square root of the noise strength.
method: The study integrates local coherence dynamics into Macroscopic Fluctuation Theory (MFT) using a framework of hydrodynamic large deviations, a two-replica transfer matrix approach, and tensor-network simulations.
model_or_system: Generic many-body quantum systems with a conserved U(1) charge, specifically modeled using 1D random charge-conserving circuits and diffusive models like the Symmetric Simple Exclusion Process (SSEP) and the Katz-Lebowitz-Spohn (KLS) model.
key_observables: Single-particle Green's function, charge density profiles, spectral gap of the quantum channel, and the many-body weight (MBW) distribution.
important_parameters: Noise strength (gamma), diffusion constant (D), mobility (sigma), and the weak-noise vs. noiseless (zero-noise) limits.
main_assumptions: The framework assumes the diffusion constant remains finite at all densities and utilizes a 'dilute coherence approximation' to treat coherences as isolated objects.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows charge profile evolution; Figure 10 displays coherence-position variance and time-dependent diffusion constants; Figure 12 and 13 show magnon number conservation breakdown and the relaxation of the MBW distribution.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of coherence decay, develops a hydrodynamic framework via MFT, analyzes the polaron formation and spectral gap in the weak-noise limit, investigates the noiseless/aging regime, and validates results via microscopic derivations and tensor-network simulations.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and open quantum systems as it provides a non-perturbative description of how dissipation and hydrodynamic fluctuations cooperatively protect quantum information through the formation of stable, collective objects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of low-entropy voids. Voids support coherent single-particle/magnon evolution of σ+. (a) In the weak-noise regime, conditional on the survival of a local co- herence (shown here as σ+), the surrounding charge profile is depleted over a finite scale ξ ∼γ−1/2. (b) In the noiseless case, the same diffusion-limited dephasing mechanism pro- duces an aging void whose width grows diffusively in time, ξ(t) ∼ √

FIG. 2. Weak-noise stationary void. (a) Top: branch-resolved density profile in the down-polarized branch, ρ(↓)(x), compared with the profile ρC(x) reconstructed from the particle-hole-symmetric diagnostic C(x, t) ≈(1 −2ρ(x, t))2 (Eq. (39)), shown here for γ = 0.04. Their agreement confirms that C(x, t) faithfully captures the branch-resolved stationary void profile. Bottom: stationary density profiles for γ ∈{0.01, · · · , 0.05}. The close agreement between the full two-replica statistical me- chanics model (“full”) and the dilute-coherence approximation (“dilute”) shows that the latter reproduces the same conditioned hydrodynamic voids. (b) Collapse of the stationary void profiles under...

FIG. 3. Noiseless aging void. (a) Conditioned density profiles ρ(x, t) at times t = 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 (reconstructed from the overlap C(x, t) defined in Eq. (41)). The density is strongly depleted near the coherence and relaxes back toward half filling over a scale that broadens with time, consistent with an aging void. (b) The same profiles plotted against the similarity variable x/ √

FIG. 4. Weak noise coherence–void polaron translation mode. (a) Mean-squared displacements of the coherence, ⟨X(t)2⟩, and of the void center of mass, ⟨xcom(t)2⟩, for several values of γ. At early times the coherence moves rapidly inside an already well-polarized quasi-stationary void, while the void center of mass diffuses much more slowly. At later times the coherence motion crosses over to match the void center of mass, indicating binding of the coherence to the slow polaron coordinate. (b) Corresponding time-dependent diffusion constants DX(γ, t) = 1

FIG. 5. Internal motion of the weak noise coherence– void polaron. (a) Mean-squared displacement of the relative-coordinate of the coherence and the void center-of- mass, ⟨(X(t) −xcom(t))2⟩, for γ = 0.007, 0.01, . . . , 0.1. In each case the relative-coordinate MSD approaches a late-time plateau, showing that the coherence remains confined inside the void, with the confinement length increasing as γ is re- duced. (b) Plateau value ℓ2 X(γ), extracted from the late-time relative-coordinate MSD, plotted against γ−2/3. The near- linear dependence is consistent with the Feynman-polaron prediction ℓζ ∼γ−1/3. These data are obtained from the same dilute-coherence population-dynamics sampling as in...

FIG. 6. Localization of the coherence–void polaron to a slow bond. (a) Late-time stationary coherence-position distribution P(X = x) in the presence of a single slow bond at x = 0. (b) Rescaled late-time position distributions plotted as γ−1/3P(X = x) against γ1/3x. The collapse is consistent with the scaling form P(X = x) ∼γ1/3f(γ1/3x), corresponding to a localization length ℓloc ∼γ−1/3. (c) Rescaled mean-squared displacement γ2/3⟨X(t)2⟩plotted against γ2/3t. The late-time saturation is consistent with the Airy scaling predicted by Eq. (97): each curve saturates at ⟨X(t)2⟩∼γ−2/3 on a timescale t ∼γ−2/3, corresponding to ℓloc ∼γ−1/3 and τloc ∼γ−2/3. Numerical data are obtained from TEBD...

FIG. 7. Subdiffusion of the zero–noise aging coherence–void polaron. (a) Phase-averaged coherence envelope |G(x, t)|2, normalized at each time and plotted against x/tα. A good collapse is obtained for α ≃0.3, with close agreement between the full two-replica statistical mechanics model (“full”) and the dilute-coherence approximation (“dilute”). (b) Second moment of the normalized coherence envelope, ⟨X(t)2⟩, showing subdiffusive growth ⟨X(t)2⟩∼t0.60 over the accessible late- time window, again with close agreement between the full model and the dilute-coherence approximation. (c) Mean-squared displacements of the coherence, the void center of mass xcom(t), and the relative coordinate...

FIG. 8. Structure of leading eigenvalues. Eigenvalues of the transfer matrix for Q ̸= 0 and Q = 0, as a function of quasimomentum k. The gaps in the two types of sectors scale parametrically differently at k = 0; however, for any nonzero k and weak enough noise, both gaps scale as √γ.

FIG. 9. Quasi-stationary decay rate estimate in translation-invariant systems. We simulate a one- dimensional gas of ballistic point particles with velocity ran- domizing collisions and a weak particle reservoir with strength γ (described in the main text) on a system of size L = 360. The tilted measure in Eq. (117) is sampled with λ = 1/2 and a = 1. Main panel: instantaneous decay rate Λ(γ, t) = −∂t log Zdet(γ, t), obtained from population-dynamics simu- lations with N = 104 clones up to time t = 2 × 104, for γ = 0.0035, . . . , 0.016. Inset: quasi-stationary plateau value ΛQSS(γ), obtained by averaging the late-time instantaneous decay rate, plotted against γ. The dashed guide shows γ1/4.

FIG. 10. Weak noise coherence–void polaron diffusion. A comparison of the time-dependent coherence diffusion in the full random-circuit dynamics (using the exact two-replica statistical mechanics model in Sec. V) and within the dilute- coherence approximation. Left: coherence-position variance ⟨X(t)2⟩for γ = 0.0025, . . . , 0.0050. Right: corresponding time-dependent diffusion constant DX(t) ≡1
